notes: Run 2026-07-08. 15 clues: 3×$200, 4×$400, 3×$600, 3×$800, 2×$1000. Subcats: Definitions (10), Etymology (3), Foreign Words & Phrases (2).

questions.clue: First proposed in a 2007 tweet by blogger Chris Messina as a way to organize posts by topic, this symbol-prefaced metadata tag was added to the Oxford English Dictionary in June 2014.

citations.title: Hashtag
citations.snippet: A hashtag is a metadata tag operator that is prefaced by the hash symbol, #. On social media, hashtags are used on microblogging and photo-sharing services–especially Twitter and Tumblr–as a form of user-generated tagging that enables cross

questions.clue: This term for a homeless, forsaken, or orphaned child — one whose gaunt appearance evokes helplessness — is similar to a ragamuffin or street urchin but emphasizes the involuntary nature of the predicament.

citations.title: Waif
citations.snippet: A waif is a person removed, by hardship, loss or other helpless circumstance, from their original surroundings. The most common usage of the word is to designate a homeless, forsaken or orphaned child, or someone whose appearance is evocati

questions.clue: This French phrase, common in formal English writing, translates literally as 'reason for being' and denotes the essential purpose that justifies a person's or organization's existence.

This nonfinite verb form — as in 'Running is good exercise,' where it functions as the subject of the sentence — simultaneously retains verb-like properties such as the ability to take a direct object.

citations.title: Gerund
citations.snippet: In linguistics, a gerund is any of various nonfinite verb forms in various languages; most often, but not exclusively, it is one that functions as a noun. The name is derived from Late Latin gerundium, meaning "which is to be carried out".

questions.clue: Its Latin name — meaning 'more than perfect' — describes a tense indicating an action completed before another past event; English speakers most often encounter it as the past perfect, as in 'she had already left.'

citations.title: Pluperfect
citations.snippet: The pluperfect, usually called past perfect in English, characterizes certain verb forms and grammatical tenses involving an action from an antecedent point in time. Examples in English are: "we had arrived" before the game began; "they had

questions.clue: This verb for a process of increasing or rising in intensity derives from the concept of a moving stairway — making it one of the unusual cases where a common English verb was formed from a mechanical conveyance.

A social group's categorical ban on something considered either excessively repulsive, sacred, or permissible only for certain members, this term encompasses restrictions ranging from dietary rules to ceremonial objects.

citations.title: Taboo
citations.snippet: A taboo is a social group's ban, prohibition or avoidance of something based on the group's sense that it is excessively repulsive, offensive, sacred or allowed only for certain people. Such prohibitions are present in virtually all societi

questions.clue: Whether governing the Latinized species names in biology or the terminology conventions of any technical discipline, this system codifies the rules for forming and using the names and terms specific to a given field.

citations.title: Nomenclature
citations.snippet: Nomenclature is a system of names or terms, or the rules for forming these terms in a particular field of arts or sciences. The theoretical field studying nomenclature is sometimes referred to as onymology or taxonymy. The principles of nam

questions.clue: Its Roman origin involved publicly posting the names of enemies of the state, subjecting them to execution and property seizure; in modern usage this Latin-derived term denotes any formal decree of condemnation, banishment, or prohibition.

citations.title: Proscription
citations.snippet: Proscription is, in current usage, a 'decree of condemnation to death or banishment' and can be used in a political context to refer to state-approved murder or banishment. The term originated in ancient Rome, where it included public ident

questions.clue: In standard English this word refers to meat from adult sheep — distinguished from 'lamb' for the first year and 'hogget' for the second — yet in South Asian and Caribbean cuisines it is routinely applied to goat meat instead.

citations.title: Lamb and mutton
citations.snippet: Lamb and mutton, collectively sheep meat, is one of the most common meats around the world. It is meat from the domestic sheep, Ovis aries, and generally divided into lamb, from sheep in their first year, hogget, from sheep in their second,

questions.clue: This South Asian language — the national tongue of Pakistan — shares its grammar, phonology, and a largely Sanskrit-derived colloquial vocabulary with Hindi, yet diverges sharply in its formal registers and is written in a Perso-Arabic script.

citations.title: Urdu
citations.snippet: Urdu is an Indo-Aryan language spoken primarily in South Asia. It is the national language and lingua franca of Pakistan. It is also an official Eighth Schedule language in India, the status and cultural heritage of which are recognised by

questions.clue: This branch of linguistics dedicated to proper names and their origins encompasses anthroponymy for personal names and surnames, toponymy for place names, and etymological analysis of how names develop and change over time.

citations.title: Nomenclature

questions.clue: The unabbreviated Latin-derived name of a tense — meaning 'more than perfect' — used for actions completed before another past event, as in 'she had already left by the time he arrived.'

citations.title: Pluperfect

questions.clue: In Spanish, this culinary term specifically designates meat from an unweaned lamb — still nursing rather than grazing — a distinction the English language has no single word for, making 'milk-fed lamb' the closest equivalent.
